physics.q: Why does a balloon-powered car move forward when air rushes out the back?
options: Every action has an equal and opposite reaction
Wind from the room pushes it
physics.explain: Newton's Third Law! Air rushing backward pushes the car forward with equal force.

physics.q: What happens to a pendulum's swing time when you make the string longer?
physics.explain: Longer pendulums have a longer period — each swing takes more time. That's how grandfather clocks keep time!

physics.q: A stretched rubber band stores what kind of energy?
physics.explain: Stretching stores elastic potential energy, which turns into kinetic (motion) energy when released.

physics.q: What force pulls a water bottle rocket back down to the ground?
physics.explain: Gravity pulls everything on Earth downward at 9.8 m/s² — rockets included!

physics.q: Wrapping more wire coils around a nail makes an electromagnet...
physics.explain: More coils = more magnetic field. Current flowing through each loop adds to the magnet's strength.

physics.q: At what angle does a launched ball travel the farthest (with no air resistance)?
physics.explain: 45° is the perfect balance between going up and going forward — try it in the Physics Playground!

physics.q: In a Rube Goldberg machine, what passes from one step to the next?
physics.explain: Each step transfers energy to the next — a rolling marble's motion energy knocks over dominoes, and so on.

physics.q: Why does a heavier balloon car move slower than a lighter one with the same balloon?
options: More mass needs more force to accelerate
physics.explain: Newton's Second Law: F = ma. With the same force, more mass means less acceleration.

chemistry.q: What gas makes the baking soda volcano erupt?
chemistry.explain: Baking soda (a base) reacts with vinegar (an acid) to make carbon dioxide gas — all those bubbles push the 'lava' out!

chemistry.q: Why does oil float on top of water?
options: Oil is less dense than water
chemistry.explain: Density is mass per volume. Oil is less dense than water, so it floats — they don't mix because oil is hydrophobic.

chemistry.q: Red cabbage juice turns RED when you add something...
chemistry.explain: Cabbage juice is a pH indicator: acids turn it red/pink, bases turn it green/blue.

chemistry.q: In the milk experiment, what does dish soap do to make the colors swirl?
chemistry.explain: Soap molecules break the milk's surface tension and chase fat molecules, dragging the food coloring around!

chemistry.q: Crystals grow from a solution when it becomes...
chemistry.explain: A supersaturated solution holds more dissolved material than it can keep — the extra comes out as beautiful crystals.

chemistry.q: Electrolysis uses electricity to split water into which two gases?
chemistry.explain: Water is H₂O — electricity breaks it into hydrogen (H₂) and oxygen (O₂) bubbles at the two pencils.

options: A change that makes new substances
chemistry.explain: In a chemical reaction, substances change into NEW substances — like vinegar + baking soda making gas that wasn't there before.

chemistry.q: Which of these is an acid?
chemistry.explain: Lemon juice contains citric acid — that's why it tastes sour and turns cabbage juice red.

biology.q: Why do plant stems grow toward light?
biology.explain: Phototropism means 'turning toward light' — plants grow toward their energy source to power photosynthesis.

biology.q: What gas does yeast produce that inflates the balloon?
biology.explain: Yeast eats sugar and releases carbon dioxide through fermentation — the same reason bread rises!

biology.q: What are the lines you see in a leaf rubbing?
options: Veins that carry water and nutrients
biology.explain: Leaf veins are like tiny highways carrying water, minerals, and food throughout the leaf.

biology.q: Why is DNA from mashed strawberries visible as white strings?
options: DNA clumps together in cold alcohol
biology.explain: DNA doesn't dissolve in cold alcohol, so it clumps into visible white strands you can spool on a toothpick!

options: They make tunnels that let in air and water
biology.explain: Worm tunnels aerate the soil and their castings add nutrients — gardeners love them!

biology.q: What do you need to see a plant cell's structure?
biology.explain: Cells are far too small for our eyes — microscopes magnify them hundreds of times so we can see nuclei and cell walls.

biology.q: What does a seed need to germinate (start growing)?
biology.explain: Seeds wake up with moisture, the right temperature, and oxygen — light matters more after the sprout appears.

biology.q: Fermentation is when microorganisms like yeast...
options: Convert sugar into energy and gas
biology.explain: Yeast metabolizes sugar for energy and releases CO₂ — used for thousands of years to make bread and more.

engineering.explain: Triangles can't be squished out of shape without changing their side lengths — that's why you see them in real bridges everywhere!

engineering.q: What does a catapult store before launch?
engineering.explain: Pulling back the spoon stretches the rubber bands, storing elastic energy that becomes motion when released.

engineering.q: In a water filter, what is each layer's job?
engineering.explain: Gravel catches big bits, sand catches smaller ones, and charcoal grabs tiny particles and chemicals.

engineering.q: An LED only lights up when the circuit is...
engineering.explain: Electricity needs a complete path from the battery, through the LED, and back — any gap stops the flow.

engineering.q: What makes a balloon rocket zoom along the string?
engineering.explain: Air rushing out the back pushes the balloon forward — thrust, just like a real rocket engine!

engineering.q: What should an engineer do when their design fails?
options: Study why it failed and improve the design
engineering.explain: Failure is data! Engineers test, learn what broke and why, redesign, and test again. That's the engineering design process.

engineering.q: Twisting the rubber band in a paper helicopter stores energy that becomes...
engineering.explain: The twisted band unwinds and spins the rotors — elastic energy becomes rotational kinetic energy, creating lift!

engineering.q: Why do engineers test bridges with weights until they break?
options: To find the weakest point and make it stronger
engineering.explain: Load testing reveals exactly where a structure fails first, so the next version can be reinforced there.
